speaker: interviewer
text: It's December 10, 1995. We were talking about you and your father being marched away by a Ukrainian. And he had your hands raised. And you knew, at that point, that he was going to shoot you.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I walked, all nothing come in mind. Mind was like blind, like nothing-- nothing. You couldn't think the evening, the day. I know only it was evening. But what happened, whatever, anything, nothing comes into your mind. Nothing makes sense. You are walking like a dumb calf to the [NON-ENGLISH], to the slaughter. And all of the sudden the Ukrainian somehow-- maybe my father didn't go fast enough, like I did. And the Ukrainian hit my father with the butt of the gun. And he hollered, [NON-ENGLISH]! You go faster! And my father fell on my back, when he hit him-- fell on my back. And he hollered, [NON-ENGLISH]! And I took off like a wind. When he start shooting, I was right between the woods. Biggest chance you have in the woods, to survive, because the trees in the woods are so big, huge, that when you ran, trees in the way. But nevertheless, one bullet grazed my shoulder. And all I didn't feel-- I felt only like somebody give me a touch. And I felt blood coming out, like this, warm, from my fingers. And I run so, like a wild animal. Because my feet was running-- I was running bare footed. I didn't have any shoes. And my feet were breaking the sticks, the twigs, on the floor. Figure that these cowards are running after me. And I run, run, run-- I don't know-- run away, far, far, far away. I don't know how long I run. All of a sudden, I run. And I hear, all of a sudden-- chug, chug, chug, chug, chug. A train is coming. And I knew for a fact my father warned me that whenever you come near a train, remember there is Romanian or Ukrainian guards, every so often, lying in bunkers, and watching the trains for the partisans. They'll kill you. And I fell in wet sweat, in a bush, in the bushes. And somehow, I fell asleep. When I got up, oblivious what has happened. And I got up, take a look, and it's dark, at night-- dark. I look between the trees. The big moon is shining. Because the day after Yom Kippur is 11 days of the month. 11 days it means the full moon is there in the sky. And here I am, running in the darkness of the woods. In daytime, it's dark. The woods stretch out deep into Russia, deep into Siberia. Because they don't stop in one place. They go-- not the cleared wooded areas. And you could imagine, I don't have a compass. I don't know where I'm going. I run without direction. I didn't even recollect where-- I don't even know how long I was running. And would you believe it, like a miracle of God, like somebody take my hand and lead me. I couldn't see the way. I had to hold the hands in the front of my face, in order I shouldn't scratch my eyes out, with the bushes hanging down from the trees. Because it's very thick woods. And lo and behold, I come back to the walk in the woods, where my father, and myself, and my brother, and other people walked to get water from the little stream there. Was in the end of the woods, on the corner. And I find my way, at night, in the darkest of the woods. The daytime is dark. And I find my way until the cave, where we were hidden, the 18 boys. Not a single body I find. I find only the shoes. I find my brother's blouse, clean blouse. And he had some photograph with him. I find them ripped apart. I find the pumpkins that we carried water with, because we didn't have any pails. But the pumpkins were all smashed. The potatoes we cooked were all smashed potatoes, smashed with the dirt mixed in between. And I was starving. Needless to say, I didn't eat anything. And I had something-- in order to survive I had to eat something. And with my fingers, my hand, I tried to pick up pieces of unbroken potatoes-- raw, unbroken. They couldn't smash them. They're even so vicious, they even smash the potatoes. But while I was lying in the woods there, and sleeping, I heard shooting, shooting, and shooting. They probably shot them someplace. Maybe they took them to the village. Maybe they tortured them. My father maybe was killed right away on the spot. I don't know. But the day when this happened is my [NON-ENGLISH] for my father. I say a kaddish, because I figured that the same day, if they didn't torture him, they probably killed him, because maybe he put a resistance. Because my father was a soldier during the First World War. Maybe they killed him there, on the spot. But I didn't find not a single body. Now my act starts. I am all alone. I have nobody, nobody, nobody-- my father, my brother. Now I have a biggest assignment of my life-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to go back-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] [CRYING] --and find my two brothers and sisters. Because I don't have nobody. And they have nobody. So [SOBBING] my determination was so great, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] that I must survive for their sake.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you thought that you would be the only one to take care of them, that you would be responsible for them, so you had to go back and try to find them hidden in the haystack, and take care of them.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, because it's only a few days expired. We left Wednesday. Anyway, I said, I must, must get back. I didn't know where I am. I didn't know how to get. But when I was with my father, walking for water, my father said, Mendel, all this way [NON-ENGLISH], here, in this corner of the world there, is our city, 17 kilometers away.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I figured I can't go on the road. I can't go on a highway. I can't go where people are driving trucks and cars. I had to go through the fields. Here I am, 24 September. Very cold at night. Why is very cold? When I start walking, I saw this field will be painted white. Because the dew become freezing. So it became white. And then the fields-- I couldn't go on the roads. I had to go in the field. The field was just cut, the summer straw, for the summer. They cut the straw. The straw is like knives, like you have razor blades on the ground. And I have my feet barefooted-- nothing on the feet.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I had to walk through the fields, bleeding. My feet were all bleeding. And I was going, going, going, full determination, not even feeling the pain. And I go, and go, and go, and walk full-heartedly. I was a strong kid. Because first of all, I had a good food yet by the [INAUDIBLE], while I worked there. And I wasn't starving in the ghetto. I was working in the fields. So the farmer fed me well. And I go, walk, walk, walk, until I come to the river. And my father told me, this river is just across the river, several kilometers, is our town. But don't go to the town.

speaker: interviewer
text: Keep to this side, to the right. Because then, you'll be seven kilometers away to the village. And when you come in the road, you'll know where you are. And I stay by the river. And I contemplate what to do. Shall I take my poor pants off, and take my shirt off, my blouse, and take it out? Or shall I swim together in my clothes?

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, if I swim with the clothes, I wouldn't be able to put dry clothes. I'll better take it off. And once I take off the clothes, and I make a bundle, and I want to go in, to go in the water, all of the sudden, the sky opened up. And a big parachute, with lights, like-- a parachute like this, but bigger, like this. And the light was burning from the bottom. It was lit up the whole area, like in daytime. I took a look. I see a bridge in front of me. Here is the river, where you sit. And here is the bridge in the front of me. And Germans with machine guns are fighting and shooting at me. And the bullets are going at me. And I fell in, into the water, with the clothes. Because the clothes I had in my hand. And I grab the clothes on one end. And I start swimming away, under the water. I start swimming away. And the bullets were getting into the water. They didn't shoot a Jew. They didn't shoot at me because I happened to be Jewish. They thought that I am a partisan. I wanted to explode, to put a bomb under the bridge. And this was my luck. Like an angel will take me by the hand and lead me, because if I would have been 100 yards on the other side of the bridge, my life would have been worth a penny.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why? Because the rapid water was going this way, away from the bridge, not to the bridge. Even so I'm a good swimmer, under no circumstance could I swim one meter against the rapids. I had to swim with the rapids, away. And when I swim away with the rapids, the bullets kept on going.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this light get up. And they shoot another one. I was already far away. And they kept on shooting, shooting. Nobody ran after me. They're big cowards, the Germans. They only shoot from far away. And I swam, swam far away. And then I said to myself, wait a minute. I have to escape. All of a sudden, I heard the roosters are crying. And the roosters crying, get day, my life is worth a penny. In daytime, they'll kill me. Because the Germans gave a proclamation. If anybody brings a Jew-- alive or dead, a boy, man, or child, whatever, or a woman-- gets a pound of sugar. The gold rush of California was not to compare. For a pound of sugar, if he wouldn't kill the child himself, he will give the child over to the Germans. He'll get a pound of sugar for it. And the Germans were so meticulously. They kept a record how many people they have killed, like it's written on the grave site-- 28,564. They count how many people are still alive, how many people they still have to catch.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anyway, I came out. And I said to myself, I took a look at the sky, and I see the Big Dipper is facing all the way down to the ground. And my father taught me-- this I'll never forget it. He was an engineer, a good engineer. And he said to me, you see, when you look up in the sky-- who could afford to have a watch in the old country?

speaker: interviewee
text: Look up in the sky. If the Big Dipper tail's to the sky, and it points with a handle to the sky, it's evening started. If it's across the heavens, like this, here is the wagon. Here is the Big Dipper, with the hand across, like this-- it means it's midnight. But if the tail is all the way down, and wagon is here, and the handle is all the way down, pointing to the ground, it means that you are near day.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I have to survive. Where do I go? There is no farms. There's no barns where I could hide. There's nothing I could hide. There's no woods. It's only clear area, near the river. And they cut the big grasses, because they made big-- and all of the sudden, I see a big haystack.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I see the haystack was a big haystack, and a platform, like you have your chair here, with pieces of wood. So the soft ground, or the wet ground, should not start corroding the grass. So they put it on the top. And this was empty. And I crawled up, under it. And I start working feverishly, naked. Because my clothes is all wet. I fell in the water with the clothes. And my body's steaming like steam. The street is cold-- 32, 30 degrees. The water is warm, because water stays warm longer. And my body is steaming like locomotive. And I am feverishly working to take out the hay, out of the bottom, to dig out a hole. And here I have the sleeve that I took my brother's shirt. And I made a knot of the sleeve. And I put in couple potatoes, in order to have something to eat on the way. And I have it with me, too. And I saved it. It didn't get lost. And I made a little hole. And I grab all the hay that I pulled out. And I take it to the water. And the water took it away. So there shouldn't be any sign that somebody dug a hole inside. And I lie inside, in daytime, inside in the haystack. And I take the shirt, together near me, in order the mice should not eat up my food.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the mice was so bold, they crawled up into my bosom, to get to the-- you could imagine what-- And I cannot turn. And I cannot do nothing. Because up, during the day, the kids are getting with the cows, on the field. And I heard kids hitting the haystack, hitting the haystack, and screaming. And I, meanwhile, slept through the day.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then there was only a quiet nothing. Everything is quiet. I stick my head out. I stick my head out, up in the sky, like this. I see the Big Dipper. It was a clear sky, the Big Dipper facing the heavens, up like this. I say, it's too early. I have to lie there a few more hours-- maybe one or two hours, until I get out at night. And I crawl over a railroad, where there was a railroad going slowly. And I came to do the walk, where the village [NON-ENGLISH] was in the way. And I came to the village. And I look at the place where I made the hole, with my father and my brother. And I crawled in, inside, with a couple potatoes, a couple apple, a cup of tomatoes. And I see a red sweater, my sister's sweater. And the kids are not there. So I said, well, maybe they walk around. They are looking for food. Because we told them not to go in daytime, only at night. They shouldn't get out in day, not to dare, but only at night.

speaker: interviewer
text: I go from one barn to the other, one barn to the other, and to the other barn. And I keep on asking, anybody, or anybody? Or maybe some Jewish man is in the barn. All of the sudden, somebody jumps off. And he was by the farmer.

speaker: interviewee
text: The farmer kept him. His name was Shlomo Berger. There was Basia Berger. And they had a son. It's only one family survived-- a son, Avrum Berger, Shlomo Berger, and Basia Berger. Three people survived as a family.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he jumped off and said, Mendel. [NON-ENGLISH]. What are you doing here? You're supposed to be in the woods. I says, I'm sorry. The Ukrainians caught us in the wood, before we met the partisans. And they killed everybody. Apparently nobody is alive. And I am all alive alone. I say, what happened to my sister, my two brothers? He say, Friday-- it means we left Wednesday. Friday, two days later, the Germans, Ukrainians surrendered the whole village. They put postings on the corners of the barns.

speaker: interviewee
text: And anybody who went out to look for food at night, they caught him. These people had a farmer taking care of them, like Shlomo Berger. He didn't have to go to look for food. He survived. So the Germans came, with a big-- Ukrainians. They took a wagon, with two horses. They put the kids on the wagon. They say the kids were very sad. One girl was crying. They got about 18 people there. All the people left, who didn't go to the woods, they caught. And between all the people were my two brothers and sister. And this when I know that I'm all alone. And then I stood there, in the village, for a couple days. And I went back in the woods. Not far from the village were wood, about six kilometers. And every so often, I came back to the village, to look for food. Summertime wasn't bad. Summertime was the whole night was free. Once the Germans made [NON-ENGLISH], and they surrounded village, and they took all the people who were there out, the rest they never bothered any more. Unless if somebody point a finger-- this and this man keeps a Jew in. But otherwise, they didn't come.

speaker: interviewer
text: So at night time, we used to come. And I remember incident when I had to-- I was starving from hunger. And I had to crawl in to a pigsty, to get some food. Because the pigs eat potatoes, and eat some food. They smash the potatoes up, as they are hot, boiling.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then they lie there for two, three days. And till today, I can't eat pizza. Because when I see pizza, the way the goo goes like this, and it reminds me the potatoes that I used to eat from the pig. One time, I go over the pigsty. And I want to crawl in, to get into-- the door was locked. But the little window's been broken open. This was winter time. This was just before Christmas time. And I heard the house, where I had to crawl into the pigsty, is Germans singing the songs. [SINGING] Silent night. I still remember that. I remember this. When I hear it, it reminds me what happened to me. And I take off my little meager little jacket. And I crawl in, inside the little window. And as soon as I crawl in, and I'm inside, a German goes out. And he wanted to relieve himself, in the corner there. And he's standing there, whistling. And I'm inside, in the pigsty. And he's near the pigsty, near me. And I picked up potatoes. The potatoes were smelly, like anything. In order for me to be able to eat to eat the potatoes, I had to take a little bit yellow salt that a farmer used to give to the horses, or to the cows, in order to mix it together, so the smells would go away. Someplace I was very lucky that I find a couple onions hanging on the wall. So I took. So the biggest survival was how to survive wintertime. When I was in the woods, and hidden in a big hole that I made out, in the ground, and when there the snow fell, my life was worth nothing. Because if a farmer would see me, going out, and looking for food-- I had to wait until the farmers came to the woods, to the cut the wood, and they walked already. And the snow has been walked, then I could go. And sometimes it passed by two or three weeks until I could I go out. Luckily, I had collected some more little potatoes, some carrots during the summer, some tomatoes I put into the haystacks. And the tomatoes-- green tomatoes. Wintertime, they got red, because lying there. But some of them were half eaten by the mice. And I didn't have a knife to cut it. Whatever is leftover, I ate it-- from the mice. Because my life was in danger. I didn't have nothing to eat. So it shows you that when you are hungry, you'll eat grass, eat bark of a tree. But the biggest happiness for me was when I was in the woods and I heard the cannon fire, coming closer, and closer, and closer, that the front is coming closer. I didn't have any connections-- nobody. Only I find a piece of paper lying on the street, on the field, to the back of the field someplace. I used to pick it up. And this was my news. I didn't know anything. But the news was the cannon fire. As soon as the cannon fire gets closer, and closer, I knew that the Russians are successful. And knew that the war is going to be ended. This gave me more courage to survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any contact at all with these Czech farmer, that had helped you before, or anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no contact at all. Because I promised him that I'll never see him. He'll never see me. And I'll never endanger his life. But the funny thing is if this Czech were to find somebody, or find a Jew, they would have chased him away. But they would not kill him. They'll tell him, go. Have pity on my family, and run away. Hide yourself. At least, let me not know that you are alive. For example, I bring out one incident that happened to me, that I would indebted to my life for this lady who would save my life.

speaker: interviewer
text: When I was hungry, starving from hunger, it was wintertime. I didn't eat bread for a few weeks, for a few months already. And I figured, what could I lose? I'll go to a woman, knock on the window, and then she give me something to eat. Maybe she'll have pity on me, give me something to eat.

speaker: interviewee
text: I knock at the window. And I speak Czech fluently. And I said to her, I'm hungry. She'd probably recognize me. Because I worked for this farm-- not for my farmer, but for another. This was another farmer. And the lady comes out-- her husband wasn't home-- and says to me, oh, my God. You're looking for food. Maybe my son is an officer in the army, in the Polish army. Maybe he is looking around the woods for food. Maybe I'll give you to eat. But at the same time, I'm afraid. Because if they catch you, and they beat you, and they torture you, you'll tell them that I gave you something to eat.

speaker: interviewer
text: So please-- you know what I'll do, she says to me. You go in, in the back of the barn. There's a big pear tree. I'll put a barrel. I'll turn the barrel over. And I'll put potatoes, [NON-ENGLISH]. I'll put a piece of bread. I'll something for you to eat. I can't do it too often, so the neighbors shouldn't see.

speaker: interviewee
text: But whenever you come here, don't even come to my house. I'm afraid for the other people. But go in the back of the barn. And any time I was hungry, I came to the back of the barn. There was potatoes. There were piece of bread. One time, she gave me a piece of black bread. And on the top was lard, this high. And I could eat lard. Because even so I'm a religious Jew, to save your life, you could eat anything. So I wasn't used to it. So I didn't want to insult her. The lady gave it to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went in the back of the barn, and took a piece of wood from the ground or somewhere, scratched everything off, everything off, scratched off the lard. Because I was starving like a skeleton. This would have been ideal for me. Because it fed.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I scratch it off, scratch it off, because I couldn't eat. This what I could tell you that had happened to me. And then I left. I'll tell you what happened to me once. One time, before Christmas, in 1944-- I was liberated a month later. I was liberated 12th of February.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was just a short time before I was liberated. I come from the woods, to the village, to look for food. At least, I'll try to sneak inside to a stable. Maybe I'll get something to eat from the pigsty. And I go over to the barn. And I want to orient myself where I am. Shall I go this way, or shall I good this way?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe I wanted to decide which way would be the best way to go. And all the sudden, like a devil comes out from nowhere, a German, dressed in a white uniform. Because it was white. There was snow all over the place. Because maybe when I walked, and the snow was very scratchy, very frozen, and probably they heard somebody walking. They didn't know that I'm-- the front came closer. And near where the front is closer, the army comes. And I didn't know. I wasn't told. And a German comes out. Here was a machine gun, like this. And I see the machine gun in front of me. And I am in the edge of the barn. Here is the barn, where I'm sitting. Here the German, from this side. And he has the machine gun. [GERMAN], like this. [GERMAN] means up there, hands up. And [GERMAN] means stop. And I took a look, and I ran in the other side. And he start shooting. While he start shooting it, I was already in the other side of the corner. And I figured if I run far, he'll catch me. Or somebody else will catch me. Because the bullets alone will bring Germans here. And I ran into the other barn, where there was a open, big door. And under the door was the utensils that the farmers used-- the [INAUDIBLE], the little-- and at the top of it is hay, and straw. This German did not think that I would run into this-- because it was exposed. But ran into the barn. Because they didn't see me running on the street. They didn't see me running nowhere. I disappeared. So all of a sudden, another German came from the other side. He was a little bit too late. In other words, they want to surround me. So one guy came too close. The other guy came a little bit late. And he didn't see me run into the other place. He came later. And I was lying there. Meanwhile, I have a jacket that I find someplace, hanging and drying.

speaker: interviewer
text: So so I figured I will cover myself something-- and I took it off, in order to make it easier for me to run. And I put it down. And I figured if anything happened, it will be lighter for me to run. And I didn't have any shoes. So I had rags on my feet. And it was very cold.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I lie under the wagon, in the barn. The barn was open. All of the sudden, I see the two Germans come up on the top. They climbed up on the top. They're on the top, on the hay, where I am lying. And I am under. Here is a little roof, for the hay. And I took off. And I ran across the street. And they heard me running. And they start shooting after me, and start running after me. And I run on the other side. And I came across, like this. I say, if I run in the field, they'll kill me. I have to hide myself in another barn. And a little black dog, a Czech dog, saved my life. I ran into a barn. And there was a little dog, lying in the barn, sleeping. And he start doing err, like this, to me. All of the sudden, he start barking. And a dog starts barking is a giveaway. Secondly, my rag-- one leg loosened up. And it showed a mark, where I'm running. In the back of the barn, nobody goes. And I ran into this barn. And I hid in the straw, like a mouse. And the dog was lying very quietly. Here the Germans are coming. They look in the barn, where I was hidden. And they open the door. And this little dog starts screaming. And he starts barking-- a little dog with a big bark. And the German said to the other, Hans-- I'll never forget the name. Hans, [GERMAN]. [GERMAN]. He didn't know that I'm a Jew. He thought I'm a partisan, I'm a spy. The [GERMAN]. [GERMAN] means the bomb. And now I find out what [GERMAN] means. I didn't know. [GERMAN]-- in German, I find what [GERMAN]. [GERMAN] is not here. But it be here [GERMAN]. [GERMAN]. [GERMAN]. [GERMAN]. And they keep saying [GERMAN], [GERMAN]. And the dog will bark. And they went out from this barn. And they went up to next door barn, to look for me. And they went to the barn. They looked around. And meanwhile, I opened up the back little door. And I ran out-- gingerly, back in the back, where I came from. And would you believe it, in order to save my life, I went back to get my jacket. Because without the jacket, I can't survive. Because it was cold. By us, the weather gets 40 below 0. 40 below 0. 40, 0, 30, 20 below 0-- not one day, but weeks and weeks. So I had to pick the jacket. And I went back to the woods.

speaker: interviewer
text: After the war, when I was liberated, I asked the farmer, what happened? They said, next day they turned over the whole barn, with the place where there was looking. They figured I'd hidden in this barn. And I was in the woods. Now the 12th of February-- Well, one other question about when you were in the woods. You had dug a hole under the ground.

speaker: interviewee
text: Dug a hole in the ground. And I put some leaves, some more kind of branches of stuff. Underground, if you dig a hole 6 feet below ground, even up at the ground is very cold, under the ground is warm. It's not warm, per se. You're not warm, like toast warm. It's not 40 degrees below 0. You could have 32 degrees-- maybe 28 degrees, but not as much as cold as upper there. You cannot make a fire, because I was afraid that with fire they could see a smoke. But I had at least support. Because I went to the villages, for the Czech villages, to look for food. There was one village seven kilometers from my home town, one village four kilometers from my home town. But the ground where I was working I knew the most, the best. There was my station, where I used to go and look for food. Because I knew the barns. I knew the people, and who's good, and who's no good, and who's catch me, but not give me over, or would not beat me, or something. So I knew they were very good people. But they were strangers, too. They were afraid for their lives. So I don't blame them. When I came there in 1998, all the village from there I wanted to come and thank them, what they saved my life. They were all moved away, to Czechoslovakia. They went back to their home country. After 300 years living in exile, they went back home.

speaker: interviewer
text: The 12th of February, I was liberated. When I was liberated, and I came to-- this time, I take a little heat. I feel myself I'm going to ask a Czech what happens. He told me, what are you sitting here? Go back to your own town. You're liberated. The Russian army is in town, in Lutsk. So I pick myself up. And I went back. Where the first thing to go? To go to see my home, where I was born. Because my father used to tell us, if the war ends-- the war ends, we should go home. We should see maybe some of us will survive. Because this is the station where we should look for-- whatever you may be, whatever you may be scattered all over. So this is another chapter, what happened when I went home. My chapter didn't end yet. So when you heard that the Russians were in Lutsk, you decided to go back there. And hopefully, you were going to hope to see if anyone else had come back.

speaker: interviewee
text: If anybody else alive.
